title: Job 40
subtitle: Job’s First Reply; God’s Second Speech Begins

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — “I Put My Hand over My Mouth”

heb.paragraph: "I am unworthy (qallōṭî) — how can I reply to you?" (v.4). Job’s first response is not repentance but silence. He puts his hand over his mouth — the ancient gesture of speechlessness. He has spoken twice (the dialogue + the oath); he will not speak again. This is not yet the full response God seeks. Silence is not repentance; it is the beginning of it.

panels.ctx: God pauses after the first speech and asks: "Will the one who contends with the Almighty correct him?" (v.2). Job’s reply is minimal: I am small; I have nothing to add; I will not speak again. This is progress from the dialogue — Job no longer demands answers — but it is not yet the transformation God intends. God will speak a second time (vv.6–41:34) to complete the process.

cross.note: "Woe to me! I am ruined! For I am a man of unclean lips" — Isaiah’s similar response to divine encounter.
"Nations will see and be ashamed... they will lay their hands on their mouths" — the hand-on-mouth gesture of awe.

notes.note: "The LORD said to Job: Will the one who contends with the Almighty correct him? Let him who accuses God answer him!" God demands a response to the first speech. Job filed a complaint; God is asking: do you still press it?

"Then Job answered the LORD: I am unworthy. I put my hand over my mouth. I spoke once, but I have no answer — twice, but I will say no more." Job is silent but not yet transformed. He acknowledges his smallness but has not yet encountered God’s goodness — only God’s power.

Calvin: the hand over the mouth is reverence, not repentance. Job is overwhelmed by God’s power but has not yet been transformed by God’s presence. The second speech will complete what the first began.
Calvin: "I will say no more" is Job choosing silence over argument. This is progress: from accusation to humility. But God wants more than silence — he wants encounter.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: the question format continues: "will the faultfinder contend with the Almighty?" The word for "faultfinder" implies someone who corrects or reproves. Job had attempted to correct God.
The NET: qallōṭî is literally "lightweight." Job acknowledges his arguments have no weight against God’s reality.

Clines: qallōṭî means "of little weight" — insignificant. Job is not confessing sin but acknowledging scale. He is small; God is vast. This is not the repentance of 42:6; it is the first step toward it.

Clines: "I spoke once... twice, but I will say no more" — Job withdraws his demand for a hearing. He no longer insists on arguing his case. But mere silence is not enough for God; the second speech will push deeper.
Habel: Job’s first response is a motion to withdraw. He does not concede guilt but abandons his demand for a hearing. The plaintiff drops the case without admitting the prosecution was right.

sections.header: Verses 6–24 — “Would You Discredit My Justice?”; Behemoth

heb.paragraph: "Look at Behemoth (bəhēmōṭ), which I made along with you" (v.15). Behemoth — possibly the hippopotamus, possibly a mythological creature, possibly both — represents raw, untameable power that God created and manages. "Along with you" — Behemoth and Job are fellow creatures. Job is no more the centre of creation than this beast is.

panels.ctx: God’s second speech opens with the sharpest question yet: "Would you discredit my justice? Would you condemn me to justify yourself?" (v.8). This is the heart of the matter. Job’s legal complaint required God to be unjust. God challenges: can YOU do what I do? Can you humble the proud, crush the wicked, bury them in the dust? (vv.10–13). If not, then God’s governance — including what seems unjust to Job — operates at a level Job cannot replicate or comprehend. Then Behemoth: God’s showcase of untameable power.

cross.note: "The LORD reigns, he is robed in majesty... the world is established, firm and secure" — the sovereignty God claims.
"Who are you, a human being, to talk back to God?" — Paul’s echo of God’s challenge to Job.

notes.note: "Then the LORD spoke to Job out of the storm: Brace yourself like a man; I will question you." God speaks again. The first speech demonstrated God’s power in creation; the second addresses justice directly.
"Would you discredit my justice? Would you condemn me to justify yourself?" The pivotal question. Job’s legal strategy required God to be in the wrong. God asks: is that really what you believe?

"Do you have an arm like God’s? Then adorn yourself with glory and splendour. Look at every proud person and humble them, crush the wicked where they stand, bury them together in the dust. Then I myself will admit to you that your own right hand can save you." The challenge: if you can do God’s job, God will acknowledge your competence. You can’t; therefore trust the one who can.

"Look at Behemoth. It ranks first among the works of God. Its bones are tubes of bronze, its limbs like rods of iron." Behemoth: God’s exhibit A of power beyond human control. Made alongside Job but vastly more powerful. If Job can’t manage Behemoth, he can’t manage the universe.

Calvin: "would you condemn me to justify yourself?" is the deepest challenge. Human self-justification often requires divine indictment. Job’s innocence seemed to require God’s guilt. God rejects the zero-sum framing.
Calvin: Behemoth demonstrates that God’s creation includes power that is terrifying and uncontrollable by humans. The lesson: trust the one who controls what you cannot.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "discredit my justice" (mishpāṭ) uses the same legal term Job used throughout. God turns Job’s own legal vocabulary against the complaint.

The NET: Behemoth may be the hippopotamus (the most dangerous animal in Africa) or a mythological chaos-beast. The Hebrew is simply bəhēmōṭ — the intensive plural of "beast," meaning the supreme beast.

Clines: this is the book’s most direct accusation against Job. Not "you sinned" but "you would condemn me to justify yourself." Job’s theology of divine injustice required God to be guilty. God rejects this framing.
Clines: "which I made along with you" is the key phrase. Behemoth is a fellow creature, not an enemy. God made it as he made Job. It serves God’s purposes, not Job’s. So does Job’s suffering.

Habel: God’s second speech addresses the legal complaint directly. The first speech said: you don’t understand creation. The second says: you don’t understand justice. Both are beyond human competence.

rows.label: The Silence — 40:1–5
rows.text: "I put my hand over my mouth." Silence, not yet repentance. The first step.

rows.label: Behemoth — 40:15–24
rows.text: God’s supreme beast. "I made it along with you." Power beyond human management.

hebtext.note: "I am unworthy (qallōṭî) — how can I reply to you?" (v.4). Job’s first response is not repentance but silence. He puts his hand over his mouth — the ancient gesture of speechlessness. He has spoken twice (the dialogue + the oath); he will not speak again. This is not yet the full response God seeks. Silence is not repentance; it is the beginning of it.

"Look at Behemoth (bəhēmōṭ), which I made along with you" (v.15). Behemoth — possibly the hippopotamus, possibly a mythological creature, possibly both — represents raw, untameable power that God created and manages. "Along with you" — Behemoth and Job are fellow creatures. Job is no more the centre of creation than this beast is.

themes.note: Sovereignty and Holiness peak: God’s second speech addresses justice directly. Justice at 8: the pivotal question reframes the legal complaint.
